content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5660 322-5660 322-3901 332-6441 322-6444

February 22, 1982

Mr. Francis LaSage
2115 E. Yosemite Avenue
Escalon, CA 95320

Dear Mr. LaSage:

Thank you for your telephone call requesting advice
from our office. As you stated to me, you are planning to
run for a City Council seat. You are currently a volunteer
reserve police officer for the City and your wife is employed
by the City as a clerk typist. Your question is whether the
Political Reform Act would prohibit you from running for
this position.

The Political Reform Act does not prohibit anyone from
running for office, rather, it deals with decision-making by
officeholders. If you are elected to the council seat, you
will be subject to the disclosure and disqualification pro-
visions of the Act.

With respect to the disqualification provisions, a
public official is forbidden from making, or participating
in the making of, or in any way attempting to use his official
position to influence a governmental decision in which he
knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest
(Government Code Section 87100). A copy enclosed). An official
has a financial interest within the meaning of Section 87100
if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have
a material financial effect on: Any source of income
(Section 87103(c)). Under the Act, "income" includes an
individual's community property interest in his spouse's
income (Section 82030(a)).

1/ All statutory references made are to the Government
Code unless otherwise noted.

2/ See the enclosed copy of Section 87103 for other
types of specified interests.

Mr. Francis LaSage
February 22, 1982
Page Two


     At first glance, it would appear that if you became a
City Councilmember you would have to abstain on any votes
concerning your wife's salary.  Section 82030(b)(2), however,
alters the outcome.  This section provides an exception
which states, "(b) Income ... does not include ... (2)
Salary ... received from a ... local ... government agency...."
Therefore, your wife's wages are exempt from the definition
of income and you have no financial interest in decisions
affecting it.

     With respect to your job as a volunteer police reserve
officer, you receive no income and, thus, have no financial
interest which would require you to abstain from participating
in decisions affecting that position or the police department.

     If you have any additional questions, please feel free
to call me at 916/322-5901.

                                        Very truly yours,


                                        [signature]
                                        Janis Shank McLean
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

JSM:km
Enclosure

sections.question_synthetic: Whether the Political Reform Act prohibits a volunteer reserve police officer whose spouse is employed by the city as a clerk typist from running for a City Council seat, and what disclosure and disqualification obligations would apply if elected.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Political Reform Act does not prohibit running for office. If elected, the person would be subject to disclosure and disqualification provisions, but would not have to abstain from votes concerning the spouse's salary because Government Code Section 82030(b)(2) exempts local government agency salaries from the definition of income, and the volunteer police position generates no income.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: No explicit 'QUESTION' or 'CONCLUSION' section headers, but document follows standard advice letter format with question stated in opening paragraphs and conclusion in response text. Footnotes and statutory references included. Enclosures referenced but not provided in text.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether the Political Reform Act prohibits a volunteer reserve police officer whose spouse is employed by the city as a clerk typist from running for a City Council seat, and what disclosure and disqualification obligations would apply if elected.

CONCLUSION: The Political Reform Act does not prohibit running for office. If elected, the person would be subject to disclosure and disqualification provisions, but would not have to abstain from votes concerning the spouse's salary because Government Code Section 82030(b)(2) exempts local government agency salaries from the definition of income, and the volunteer police position generates no income.

The Political Reform Act does not prohibit anyone from running for office, rather, it deals with decision-making by officeholders. With respect to the disqualification provisions, a public official is forbidden from making, or participating in the making of, or in any way attempting to use his official position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest (Government Code Section 87100). Mr. Francis LaSage February 22, 1982 Page Two At first glance, it would appear that if you became a City Councilmember you would have to abstain on any votes concerning your wife's salary. embedding.summary: FPPC advises that the Political Reform Act does not prohibit candidacy for City Council, but explains the disqualification provisions that would apply if elected, clarifying that the candidate would not face financial interest conflicts regarding either the spouse's city employment or the volunteer police position.
